notes: Headquartered Maynard MA; $11B+ revenue FY1988; 120000+ employees; primary subject of document. Known as Digital internally.
President and co-founder of DEC; delivered keynote 'One Company Theme and Digital's Evolving Strategy'; MIT-trained engineer; forced out by board 1992
Vice President Finance at DEC; delivered Financial Update speech; reported 20% revenue growth, 13% pre-tax margins, $2B cash
Vice President Product Strategy and Architecture at DEC; delivered 'Digital's Evolving Product Strategy'; chief architect of six-pillar enterprise strategy
Manager, Low-End Systems Engineering at DEC; delivered desktop strategy speech; championed terminal and workstation products
Corporate Consulting Engineer at DEC; delivered DECwindows Solution speech; architect of DECwindows/X Windows strategy. Later joined Microsoft.
Manager, Database Systems PBU at DEC; delivered 'OLTP and Database Products' speech; explained transaction processing architecture and market
Vice President, High Performance Systems at DEC; delivered 'Our Transaction Processing Strategy'; lead executive for DECtp announcement
Manager, Advanced High-End Systems Development Group at DEC; delivered 'Role of High Availability in Transaction Processing'; VAXcluster architecture expert
Vice President, Corporate Systems Group at DEC; delivered 'Meeting Customers' Needs — Digital's Distributed Production Systems'; competitive analysis vs IBM/HP/Tandem
Marketing executive at DEC who led the work behind the DECtp announcement covered in this document; context entity, not a speaker. Later co-founded Aberdeen Group.
DEC executive; gave separate State of the Company presentation on 'Research as a Market — a Window of Opportunity'; summary deferred to next MGMT MEMO issue
Editor of MGMT MEMO internal DEC newsletter; later preserved and published DEC internal documents at seltzerbooks.com
Primary competitor; cited as dominant in mainframe/batch TP, SNA networking; characterized as having multiple OS/TP/DB systems, high cost of ownership, no distributed TP
Named competitor; cited as strong in lab/science markets, RISC architecture; criticized for four OS platforms, no TP applications on Unix, limited networking
Named TP market leader; praised for marketing and distributed relational DB; criticized for proprietary language/OS, narrow TP focus, poor multi-vendor networking
Named partner; DEC announced joint development with Apple to integrate Macintosh into enterprise-wide computing environment
Named competitor in workstation market; DEC claimed it surpassed Sun's growth rate in FY1988
Named workstation competitor; DEC claimed it leaped over Apollo to become number two in workstations
Named via MS-DOS and OS/2 trademarks; DEC committed to integrating MS-DOS and OS/2 environments into NAS strategy
Named as trademark holder of Unix; DEC acknowledged Unix origins on PDP-7 and PDP-11 systems
Named as NAS architecture integration target for high-end technical and scientific computing
Referenced as origin of interactive computing ideas; Olsen cited MIT connections; X Windows emerged from Project Athena collaboration with DEC at MIT
